title: Svapna History — Interactive Timeline Data
description: Dated events, figures, and works across the six units of the History Module, ready to drop into the site's timeline component. Dates are CE unless prefixed BCE; circa-dates are marked with c. Each entry has a unit reference for filtering.

entries.title: Ṛgveda composed
entries.summary: Early Vedic dream references, principally apotropaic verses against bad dreams (RV 10.36.4, 8.47.14–18). Establishes dream as a real domain of ritual concern in Vedic India.

entries.title: Shang oracle bones with dream divinations
entries.summary: Late Shang dynasty scapulimancy includes systematic royal dream-questions. State-level dream divination established in China.

entries.title: Atharvaveda dream-omen passages
entries.summary: Atharvaveda 6.46, 7.100, 16.5 systematize dream-omens with ritual responses for bad dreams.

entries.title: Homer, Iliad and Odyssey
entries.summary: Earliest Greek treatments of dream as message from the gods. Penelope's gates of horn and ivory (Od. 19.560–569) introduce the epistemic problem of dream.

entries.title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad composed
entries.summary: Yājñavalkya–Janaka dialogue (Book IV) — the dreamer 'fashions for itself' the dream from waking impressions. First sustained Indian philosophy of dream.

entries.title: Chāndogya Upaniṣad composed
entries.summary: First clear treatment of deep sleep (suṣupti) as a state of consciousness in its own right (CU 6.8 — the bird-and-string analogy).

entries.title: Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad composed
entries.summary: Twelve verses establishing the four-state model (jāgrat / svapna / suṣupti / turīya) and pairing each state with the syllable Om. The philosophical bedrock for nearly all later Asian discussion of consciousness across states.

entries.title: Zhuangzi composed
entries.summary: Zhuang Zhou (369–286 BCE). The butterfly passage in Inner Chapters 2 — the most-cited Chinese text on dream. Reframes the dream–waking question around 'the transformation of things' (wu hua).

entries.title: Aristotle's On Sleep, On Dreams, On Divination through Sleep
entries.summary: The Parva Naturalia — first systematic Greek philosophy of dream. Naturalistic, skeptical of most prophetic dreams, treats dream as residual perceptual activity.

entries.title: Zhouli describes the zhanmeng office
entries.summary: Court official charged with dream-interpretation, with a six-fold classification (ordinary, surprise, thinking, waking, joyful, fearful dreams).

entries.title: Milindapañha composed
entries.summary: The dialogues of Nāgasena and the Bactrian Greek king Menander (Pāli: Milinda). Book IV's dream passages establish the four-cause typology (vāta, pitta, kapha, perceptual residues) influential across centuries of Buddhist scholasticism.

entries.title: Cicero, De Divinatione
entries.summary: Sustained skeptical critique of dream-prophecy and Roman divination tradition. An unusually rigorous ancient treatment of the epistemology of dream-revelation.

entries.title: Artemidorus, Oneirocritica
entries.summary: Five-book interpretive manual — the most complete Greek dream-book to survive. The structural ancestor of every dream-dictionary published since.

entries.title: Galen, On Diagnosis from Dreams
entries.summary: Hippocratic-Galenic medical tradition's developed treatment of dream as diagnostic indicator. Will dominate Mediterranean and Islamic medical thinking about dream into early modern period.

entries.title: Liezi compiled
entries.summary: Includes Chapter 3 'King Mu of Zhou' — most developed early Daoist dream theory. Spirit-cultivation framework: dream-state and waking-state in bidirectional feedback through cultivation of qi and shen.

entries.title: Asaṅga and Vasubandhu found Yogācāra
entries.summary: Brothers who develop Vijñānavāda Buddhism. Vasubandhu's Viṃśatikā becomes locus classicus for Buddhist analysis of dream as illustrating the constructed character of all experience (vijñapti-mātra).

entries.title: Macrobius, Commentary on the Dream of Scipio
entries.summary: Five-fold dream classification (somnium, visio, oraculum, insomnium, visum) becomes canonical in medieval European tradition for the next millennium.

entries.title: Buddhaghosa's Visuddhimagga
entries.summary: Theravāda commentarial tradition's framework for dream — four causes (refining the Milindapañha scheme). Treats dream as illustration of conditioned experience rather than as object of specific cultivation.

entries.title: Shōtoku Taishi (574–622)
entries.summary: Foundational figure of Buddhist statecraft in Japan; associated with the yume-dono (dream-hall) tradition at Hōryū-ji.

entries.title: Gauḍapāda's Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā
entries.summary: Verse commentary on the Mandukya. Chapter 3 develops the philosophical argument that waking and dream have parallel logical status — the engine that the Tibetan illusory-body tradition will later operationalize.

entries.title: Muḥammad Ibn Sirin (c. 654–728)
entries.summary: Basran scholar associated with the systematic Islamic dream-interpretation tradition. The textual corpus bearing his name is the foundation of the dream-science (ilm tafsīr al-ru'yā) for the Islamic world.

entries.title: Padmasambhava and the early transmission
entries.summary: Tantric teacher from Oḍḍiyāna brought to Tibet under King Trisong Detsen. Foundational figure for the Nyingma tradition; the early translation period (snga 'gyur) begins with his work.

entries.title: Kūkai / Kōbō Daishi (774–835)
entries.summary: Brings esoteric Buddhism to Japan from Tang China. Sokushin Jōbutsu Gi establishes framework of three mysteries (sanmitsu) within which Shingon dream practice operates.

entries.title: Tang dynasty chuanqi dream-tales flourish
entries.summary: Shen Jiji's Pillow Story (c. 781) crystallizes the huang liang yi meng (yellow-millet dream) trope: an entire life experienced in a dream during the time it takes to cook a pot of millet.

entries.title: Chen Tuan (c. 871–989)
entries.summary: Daoist master associated with Mount Hua and the sleep-cultivation tradition. The Twenty-Four Sleep Exercises (Mian gong er-shi-si shi) develop a structured Daoist sleep-yoga, preserved later in the Quanzhen lineage.

entries.title: Tilopa (988–1069)
entries.summary: Indian mahāsiddha who synthesized teachings from multiple Indian sources into what would become the Six Yogas. First link in the Kagyu transmission chain.

entries.title: Inner alchemy (neidan) tradition matures
entries.summary: Tang–Song development of jing/qi/shen cultivation, the reversal of natural flow, and the embryo of immortality. The framework within which Chen Tuan's sleep practices are situated.

entries.title: Nāropa (956–1041)
entries.summary: Tilopa's principal disciple. Systematizes Tilopa's teachings into the Six Yogas of Naropa: tummo, illusory body, dream, clear light, bardo, transference.

entries.title: Marpa the Translator (1012–1097)
entries.summary: Travels to India three times, studies with Nāropa, brings the Six Yogas back to Tibet. Founding figure of the Kagyu lineage.

entries.title: Second diffusion (phyi dar) consolidates
entries.summary: Tibetan students travel to India; Indian masters invited to Tibet; new translations produced. Institutional framework for the four major schools begins to take shape.

entries.title: Padampa Sangye (d. 1117)
entries.summary: Indian master who transmitted zhi byed (pacifying) and the gcod (chöd) tradition to Tibet, including distinctive dream practices.

entries.title: Milarepa (c. 1040–1123)
entries.summary: Marpa's principal disciple. The great yogi-poet whose songs (mgur 'bum) include some of the most beautiful Tibetan literature on dream and sleep practice.

entries.title: Khyungpo Naljor and the Niguma transmission
entries.summary: Founder of the Shangpa Kagyu. Brings back the Six Yogas of Niguma — a parallel transmission to the Nāropa lineage.

entries.title: Machig Labdrön (1055–1149)
entries.summary: Female master who systematized chöd practice. The chöd dream-practice (offering the dream-body to dream-beings) is a distinctive Tibetan contribution.

entries.title: Gampopa (1079–1153)
entries.summary: Milarepa's principal disciple. Unites the Kagyu yogic tradition with the Kadampa monastic tradition. Author of Jewel Ornament of Liberation.

entries.title: Suhrawardī (1154–1191)
entries.summary: Persian philosopher of illuminationism (ḥikmat al-ishrāq). Develops the framework of the 'ālam al-mithāl al-mutlaq (absolute imaginal world). Executed at thirty-seven.

entries.title: First Karmapa, Düsum Khyenpa (1110–1193)
entries.summary: Founder of the Karma Kagyu lineage — the first formally-recognized tulku (reincarnated teacher) lineage in Tibet.

entries.title: Myōe Kōben (1173–1232) and his dream diary
entries.summary: The Yume no Ki — a continuous dream-record kept for nearly forty years, among the longest sustained dream-records in any pre-modern tradition. Treats dream as continuous with waking practice and as a field of devotional encounter.

entries.title: Ibn al-ʿArabī (1165–1240)
entries.summary: The Andalusian Sufi master ('al-Shaykh al-Akbar'). Develops the metaphysics of imagination and the doctrine of the 'ālam al-mithāl (imaginal world) as a real ontological domain. Futūḥāt al-Makkiyya and Fuṣūṣ al-Ḥikam are the principal works.

entries.title: Dōgen composes 'Mu chū setsu mu'
entries.summary: The Shōbōgenzō fascicle 'Within a Dream Expressing the Dream'. Among the most philosophically sophisticated treatments of dream in any tradition. Reframes the dream/reality distinction as the activity of buddha-nature.

entries.title: Dōgen (1200–1253) dies
entries.summary: Founder of Sōtō Zen in Japan. The Shōbōgenzō stands as his masterwork and the most sophisticated Zen treatment of dream and waking.

entries.title: Bardo Thödol cycle revealed by Karma Lingpa (14th century)
entries.summary: The 'Tibetan Book of the Dead' tradition — termas attributed to Padmasambhava but revealed by the tertön Karma Lingpa. Includes the dream-bardo passages central to the Tibetan dream-yoga inheritance.

entries.title: Naqshbandī Sufi order founded
entries.summary: Central Asian Sufi order known for distinctive dream practices, including structured engagement with dreams of the deceased master.

entries.title: Tsongkhapa (1357–1419)
entries.summary: Founder of the Geluk school. His commentaries on the Six Yogas of Nāropa are among the most rigorous Tibetan treatments of dream practice.

entries.title: Mullā Ṣadrā (1571–1640)
entries.summary: Great Safavid-era Persian philosopher. Integrates the Akbarian (Ibn al-ʿArabī) framework with peripatetic philosophy and the Shi'ite tradition.

entries.title: Hervey de Saint-Denys, Les rêves et les moyens de les diriger
entries.summary: First systematic Western study of deliberate dream-cultivation. Based on a 1,946-night dream journal. Develops induction methods that anticipate nearly every modern lucid-dreaming technique.

entries.title: Freud, Die Traumdeutung (The Interpretation of Dreams)
entries.summary: Establishes the depth-psychological framework that will dominate Western thinking about dream for most of the twentieth century. Treats dream as coded message to be analyzed, not as a state in which one practices.

entries.title: Jamgon Kongtrul Lodrö Thaye (1813–1899)
entries.summary: Great non-sectarian (ris med) compiler of the nineteenth century. Treasury of Knowledge and Treasury of Spiritual Instructions are encyclopedic compilations including substantial Six Yogas materials across multiple lineages.

entries.title: Frederik van Eeden, 'A Study of Dreams'
entries.summary: Lecture to the Society for Psychical Research, December 19, 1913; published 1914. Coins the term 'lucid dream' (Dutch: helder bewust droom) as a technical category. Based on van Eeden's own dream journal (1898–1912).

entries.title: Mary Arnold-Forster, Studies in Dreams
entries.summary: First book-length English-language treatment of deliberate dream cultivation aimed at general readers. Develops the flying technique for dream-stabilization.

entries.title: Breton, Manifeste du surréalisme
entries.summary: Surrealist movement places dream at the center of avant-garde artistic project. Develops techniques (automatic writing, sleep-induced composition) for cultivating dream-like states in waking creative work.

entries.title: Ouspensky, A New Model of the Universe
entries.summary: Chapter 6 'Experimental Mysticism' describes 'half-dream state' experiments — essentially a wake-initiated lucid dreaming (WILD) technique. Bridges empirical and contemplative-philosophical traditions.

entries.title: Evans-Wentz, Tibetan Yoga and Secret Doctrines
entries.summary: First English translation of Six Yogas of Naropa material. Editorially flawed (theosophical contamination) but the first sustained Western access to the Tibetan dream-yoga tradition.

entries.title: Aserinsky and Kleitman discover REM sleep
entries.summary: 'Regularly Occurring Periods of Eye Motility' (Science 118: 273–274). Establishes that the sleeping brain cycles through distinct states with distinct neurophysiological signatures. Founds the modern science of sleep.

entries.title: Paul Tholey begins lucid-dream research
entries.summary: Frankfurt sport scientist and Gestalt psychologist. Develops the reflection technique (Reflexionstechnik) — the cultivation of habitual reality-testing in waking that transfers into dream. Most empirically validated induction method.

entries.title: Dalai Lama and senior teachers flee Tibet
entries.summary: Chinese occupation forces emigration of Tibetan religious leadership. Unintended consequence: Tibetan teachers come into contact with Western students at scale for the first time.

entries.title: Namkhai Norbu settles in Italy
entries.summary: Begins what will become the Dzogchen Community — the principal Western institutional channel for Dzogchen dream-yoga teaching.

entries.title: Chögyam Trungpa Rinpoche arrives in England
entries.summary: Karma Kagyu and Nyingma trained; among the first Tibetan teachers to settle in the West and teach openly to Western students. Moves to North America in 1970.

entries.title: Henry Corbin, Creative Imagination in the Sufism of Ibn 'Arabi
entries.summary: Foundational Western treatment of the Sufi imaginal-world tradition. Translates 'ālam al-mithāl as mundus imaginalis. Opens the Western reception of Ibn al-ʿArabī's framework.

entries.title: Hearne records the first lucid-dream eye-movement signal
entries.summary: University of Hull. Alan Worsley, in confirmed REM sleep, executes the agreed left-right-left-right eye-movement sequence — the first objective verification that a sleeping person can communicate with the waking world during a dream.

entries.title: LaBerge's Stanford verification
entries.summary: Stephen LaBerge independently verifies lucid dreaming at Stanford using the eye-movement signal paradigm. Published 1981 in Perceptual and Motor Skills. The paper that opens the modern scientific era for the broader research community.

entries.title: LaBerge, Lucid Dreaming
entries.summary: First popular book on the science. Begins the cultural diffusion of the modern lucid-dreaming framework.

entries.title: Mind and Life Institute founded
entries.summary: Francisco Varela, Adam Engle, and the Dalai Lama. Establishes the principal venue for sustained dialogue between Buddhist contemplatives and Western scientists. Will produce the foundational integrative texts of the convergence.

entries.title: LaBerge founds The Lucidity Institute
entries.summary: Institutionalizes lucid-dreaming research and education. Will be the principal Western lucid-dreaming research center for the following decades.

entries.title: LaBerge and Rheingold, Exploring the World of Lucid Dreaming
entries.summary: Becomes the standard introductory text for the contemplative-scientific reception of lucid dreaming. The course's Module 12 reading.

entries.title: Varela, Thompson, Rosch, The Embodied Mind
entries.summary: Foundational integrative text bringing Buddhist philosophy into dialogue with cognitive science. The framework within which the contemporary contemplative-scientific synthesis develops.

entries.title: Tenzin Wangyal Rinpoche founds Ligmincha International
entries.summary: Principal Western institutional channel for the Bön Mother Tantra dream-yoga tradition.

entries.title: Mind and Life dialogue on sleep, dreaming, dying
entries.summary: Held with the Dalai Lama and a group of senior scientists and contemplatives. Will produce Sleeping, Dreaming, and Dying (1997).

entries.title: Varela ed., Sleeping, Dreaming, and Dying
entries.summary: The Mind and Life dialogue volume. The single best entry point to the contemplative-scientific integration on dream practice. Essential reading.

entries.title: Tenzin Wangyal, Tibetan Yogas of Dream and Sleep
entries.summary: The most-read English text on dream-yoga practice. The course's Modules 2, 3, and 7 primary reading.

entries.title: Namkhai Norbu, Dream Yoga and the Practice of Natural Light (revised edition)
entries.summary: Primary modern Dzogchen treatment of dream practice in English. The course's Module 3 secondary reading.

entries.title: Voss et al., 'Lucid Dreaming: A State of Consciousness with Features of Both Waking and Non-Lucid Dreaming'
entries.summary: Sleep 32: 1191–1200. High-resolution EEG study establishing that lucid dreaming exhibits a hybrid neural signature — gamma activity of waking plus REM substrate. Major step in the maturation of the science.

entries.title: Dresler et al., dorsolateral prefrontal cortex activation in lucid REM
entries.summary: Sleep 35: 1017–1020. Combined EEG/fMRI localizes the lucid-dreaming signature to the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex — the metacognitive monitoring region whose activity is suppressed in ordinary REM sleep.

entries.title: B. Alan Wallace, Dreaming Yourself Awake
entries.summary: Most-developed contemplative-scientific bridge text for dream-yoga. Integrates the Tibetan tradition with contemporary cognitive science.

entries.title: Voss et al., gamma-tACS induction study
entries.summary: Nature Neuroscience 17: 810–812. Transcranial alternating current stimulation at 25 and 40 Hz applied during REM sleep increases lucid-dream rates. Controversial; mixed replication.

entries.title: Evan Thompson, Waking, Dreaming, Being
entries.summary: Most rigorous contemporary philosophical treatment of consciousness across the four states. Successor volume to Sleeping, Dreaming, and Dying.

entries.title: Filevich et al., metacognition–lucid-dreaming structural correlation
entries.summary: Journal of Neuroscience 35: 1082–1088. Frequent lucid dreamers show enhanced gray matter volume in anterior prefrontal cortex — the brain region central to metacognitive monitoring. Ties lucid dreaming to general metacognitive capacity.

entries.title: Andrew Holecek, Dream Yoga
entries.summary: Accessible integration of the Tibetan tradition with contemporary lucid-dreaming science. The course's Module 5 secondary reading.

entries.title: Aspy et al., reality-testing + MILD induction study
entries.summary: Dreaming 27: 206–231. National Australian Lucid Dream Induction Study — establishes comparative effectiveness of major induction techniques (WBTB + MILD + reality-testing) in a substantial sample.

entries.title: LaBerge et al., galantamine pharmacological induction study
entries.summary: PLOS ONE 13: e0201246. Double-blind placebo-controlled trial — 8 mg pre-sleep galantamine produces ~4× increase in lucid-dream rates. The first robustly-replicated pharmacological lucid-dream induction.

entries.title: Namkhai Norbu (1938–2018) dies
entries.summary: Senior figure of Western Dzogchen transmission for the second half of the twentieth century. Continues through the Dzogchen Community.

entries.title: Holzinger et al., lucid-dream treatment for nightmares (clinical)
entries.summary: Behavioral Sleep Medicine 18: 462–474. Clinical study establishing lucid-dreaming-based intervention as an effective therapeutic tool for chronic nightmare disorder, particularly when integrated with imagery rehearsal therapy.

entries.title: Stickgold and Zadra, When Brains Dream
entries.summary: Most recent accessible synthesis of the contemporary science of dreaming, including lucid dreaming.
